input_candidates.truth_value: Investigation of the effect of 101 mass ppm hydrogen on the room temperature deformation structure and mechanical behavior of an austenitic Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C (wt.%) low-density steel, focusing on hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB) and hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB) mechanisms and their role in high hydrogen embrittlement resistance.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fully austenitic (fcc) with average grain size 175 μm. After tensile deformation to ε=0.4 with 101 mass ppm H, dislocation structures exhibit hydrogen-enhanced deformation banding (HEDB) and hydrogen-enhanced microbanding (HEMB). Cell blocks show increased GNB misorientation and localized microbands along GNBs. Type 2 regions develop macroscopic kink bands (10-30 μm thick) and refined dislocation cells. Type 3 regions show extensive dense dislocation walls, microbands, and submicron deformation bands. No ordered κ-carbides detected.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fully austenitic fcc structure with average grain size 175 μm. Hydrogen promotes HEDB (macroscopic kink bands 10-30 μm and submicron DBs) and HEMB (MBs nucleated along GNBs and within DBs). DC size refined to 192 ± 10 nm (Type 2) and 212 ± 10 nm (Type 3). GNB misorientations increased. No ordered κ-carbides detected.

input_candidates.truth_value: Fully austenitic (fcc) with average grain size 175 μm. After tensile deformation to ε=0.4, dislocation structures consist of cell blocks (Type 1a,1b), dislocation cells (Type 2), and a mixture of cells, dense dislocation walls, and microbands (Type 3) depending on grain orientation relative to tensile axis.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fully austenitic fcc structure with average grain size 175 μm. After tensile deformation the dislocation structures include Type 1a cell blocks (GNB1, GNB2, IDBs), Type 1b cell blocks (two GNB sets), Type 2 dislocation cells (average 220 ± 10 nm), and Type 3 complex structures (DCs ~236 ± 10 nm, DDWs, MBs 150-200 nm thick).

input_candidates.truth_value: Hydrogen desorption curve (Fig. 1) shows a single peak, maximum desorption rate 0.58 mass ppm/min at 700 K.
input_candidates.prediction_value: True fracture stress (TFS) measured as 825 ± 15 MPa for the uncharged sample and 845 ± 15 MPa for the hydrogen-charged sample (Table 1).

input_candidates.truth_value: Table 1: Uncharged sample: True fracture stress (TFS) = 825 ± 15 MPa, Uniform elongation (UEL) = 0.43 ± 0.01, Elongation-to-failure (ETF) = 0.51 ± 0.01. Hydrogen-charged sample: TFS = 845 ± 15 MPa, UEL = 0.46 ± 0.02, ETF = 0.55 ± 0.01.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Stacking fault energy (SFE) of the steel is 64 ± 10 mJ/m² at 293 K.

input_candidates.truth_value: Strain-hardening stages A-D observed in both samples; hydrogen does not introduce new hardening stage but increases strain-hardening rate at early stages (Fig. 2b).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Engineering stress-strain data from Figure 2: uncharged sample stress rises from 270 MPa at ε=0 to 560 MPa at ε=0.4; hydrogen-charged sample rises from 280 MPa to 580 MPa, with both softening beyond ε=0.4.

input_candidates.truth_value: Hydrogen increases yield stress by ~27 MPa (10% increase) and flow stress by 14-25 MPa; ductility increases (UEL from 0.43 to 0.46, ETF from 0.51 to 0.55).
input_candidates.prediction_value: True stress-strain and strain-hardening curves from Figure 2 exhibit four hardening stages (A-D) for both conditions; strain-hardening values at true strains 0.00, 0.05, 0.15, 0.25, 0.35 are approximately 300, 1600, 1900, 1500, 800 MPa.

input_candidates.truth_value: Type 1a CB in uncharged: primary GNB system, GNB misorientation distribution; hydrogen shifts GNB distribution to higher angles, increasing average θ_GNB^H by ~1° and extending distribution up to 7-8°. IDB misorientation increase ~0.25° (Fig. 7).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Quantitative misorientation analysis (Figure 7): hydrogen increases average GNB misorientation by ~1°; Type 1b MB misorientations in hydrogen-charged sample reach 5.0 ± 0.2° and 5.6 ± 0.2°.

input_candidates.truth_value: Type 1b CB: hydrogen increases GNB1 and GNB2 misorientations similarly, population of high-angle boundaries enhanced.
input_candidates.prediction_value: Dislocation cell size distributions (Figures 11 and 16): Type 2 DC size refined from 220 ± 10 nm to 192 ± 10 nm; Type 3 DC size refined from 236 ± 10 nm to 212 ± 10 nm; Type 3 cell boundary misorientation remains nearly unchanged (~0.87° vs ~0.90°).

input_candidates.truth_value: GNB misorientations <2° correspond to dense dislocation walls (DDWs) with thickness ~90 nm; >2° associated with microbands (MBs) of thickness 150-200 nm, length 1.5-4 μm (Fig. 8).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Comparative HE susceptibility index (IHE) from Table 2: Fe30Mn6.5Al0.3C exhibits IHE = -0.045 (HEMB mechanism) at ~104 mass ppm H, comparable to high HE-resistant alloys such as 310S (IHE = -0.11, HEDT) and superior to 304 (IHE = 0.6) and 316 (IHE = 0.14).

input_candidates.truth_value: Hydrogen refines average dislocation cell size in Type 2 structures from 220 ± 10 nm to 192 ± 10 nm (Fig. 11).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Hydrogen desorption curve (Figure 1) shows a single peak with maximum rate 0.58 mass ppm/min at 700 K, total measured hydrogen 101 mass ppm.

input_candidates.truth_value: Type 3: hydrogen promotes dense dislocation walls, deformation bands, and microbands; cell size refined from 236 ± 10 nm to 212 ± 10 nm; cell boundary misorientation only slightly affected (0.87° to 0.90°) (Fig. 16).
input_candidates.prediction_value: Fractography (Figure 17): both conditions show fully ductile dimple fracture; hydrogen refines dimple size from 2-8 μm (uncharged) to 1.5-3.0 μm (charged) via void sheets from enhanced strain localization.
